title: Chewing Gum on Wool
stainChemistry: Chewing gum is a hydrophobic polymer blend (polyisobutylene, polyvinyl acetate) mixed with wax, softeners, and sugar or sugar alcohols. On wool, the gum adheres to the surface scales of the keratin fiber and can embed into the pile. The polymer base does not dissolve in water. Freezing makes the gum brittle and breaks the adhesive bond with the fiber, allowing mechanical removal without pulling or damaging wool fibers.
whyThisWorks: Freezing hardens the gum polymer from a pliable adhesive into a brittle solid. Once frozen, the gum can be scraped or cracked off the wool without pulling fibers. Any residual gum film is dissolved with a small amount of POG or dry-cleaning solvent. Wool requires cold water only throughout — heat softens gum further and felts the wool fiber.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply a small amount of POG (Paint-Oil-Grease remover) or dry-cleaning solvent to any remaining gum residue. Dab gently with a white cloth — do not rub or scrub the wool pile. The solvent dissolves the polymer film without water. Blot dry.

spottingProtocol.technique: Gentle blot to remove solvent residue
spottingProtocol.instruction: Dampen a clean white cloth with cold water and a small amount of NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent). Blot the treated area to remove any solvent residue. Do not soak the wool — keep the area damp, not wet. Blot dry with a clean towel.

homeSolutions: Test on a hidden area first (inside seam or hem) — wool dyes may react to cleaning agents.
Freeze the gum with ice cubes in a plastic bag (15-30 min) or put the garment in the freezer. Once frozen solid, scrape off with a butter knife or credit card edge.

For any remaining gum residue, apply a tiny amount of peanut butter or cooking oil to soften it, then wipe gently with a cloth. Follow with cold water and a drop of wool-safe detergent to remove the oil.
If gum is deeply embedded in the wool pile, take to a professional dry cleaner — aggressive scraping risks pulling wool fibers.
materialWarnings: ALWAYS test on a hidden seam or inside hem first — wool dyes may not tolerate spotting agents.
NEVER use hot water or heat — heat softens gum (spreading it deeper) and felts wool fiber
NEVER rub or scrub warm gum — it smears into the wool pile and bonds tighter
NEVER use chlorine bleach on wool — destroys keratin permanently
NEVER use acetone or nail polish remover on wool — may dissolve dyes

escalation.when: If gum is deeply embedded in a hand-knotted or antique wool rug, or if scraping has already damaged the pile
escalation.whatToTell: Chewing gum on wool — frozen and scraped but residue remains embedded in the pile. Please use dry-side solvent approach, no heat.
escalation.specialistType: professional dry cleaner or wool rug specialist

household.name: Ice cubes (in plastic bag)
household.note: Freeze the gum solid before scraping. Essential first step.

household.name: Goo Gone
household.note: Dissolves gum residue after bulk removal. Use sparingly on wool — test first.

professional.name: POG (Paint-Oil-Grease Remover)
professional.note: Professional solvent for gum/adhesive residue. Wool-safe in small amounts.

customerExplanation: Chewing gum is a polymer that sticks to wool fibers. We froze it solid so it became brittle and could be scraped off cleanly without damaging the wool. Any remaining film was dissolved with a gentle solvent. The wool pile was brushed back into shape after treatment.
commonMistakes: Trying to pull or pick warm gum off wool — this pulls fibers and embeds the gum deeper
Using hot water or a hair dryer — heat softens gum and felts wool simultaneously
Over-applying solvent — a little goes a long way on wool
aftercare: Freeze the gum: place a bag of ice directly on the gum for 15-30 minutes, or place the entire garment in a plastic bag in the freezer for 30 minutes. The goal is to make the gum completely hard and brittle. Do not skip this step — attempting to scrape warm gum off wool will pull fibers and damage the pile. While the gum is still frozen and brittle, scrape it off with a dull edge. Work from the outside of the gum toward the center. The frozen gum should crack and lift off in pieces. If the gum softens during scraping, re-freeze before continuing. On wool, scrape with the pile direction to avoid fiber damage.
